title: Galerkin Methods and Finite Elements
objectives: Understand the weak formulation of boundary value problems
Apply the Galerkin method with various basis functions
Develop the finite element method as a Galerkin method with local basis
Construct element stiffness matrices and assemble global systems

content.text:
The **method of weighted residuals** provides a unifying framework for many numerical methods. Given a BVP $\mathcal{L}[y] = f$ with an approximate solution $y_N$, the **residual** is:
\[
R(x) = \mathcal{L}[y_N](x) - f(x)
\]

content.title: Method of Weighted Residuals
content.content:
Choose $N-1$ **test functions** (or weight functions) $\{w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_{N-1}\}$ and require:
\[
\int_a^b w_i(x) R(x)\, dx = 0, \quad i = 1, \ldots, N-1
\]
The residual is forced to be orthogonal to each test function.

content.text: Different choices of test functions yield different methods:

content.title: Galerkin Method
content.content:
Let $y_N(x) = \sum_{j=1}^{N-1} c_j \phi_j(x)$ where each $\phi_j$ satisfies homogeneous boundary conditions. The **Galerkin method** requires:
\[
\int_a^b \phi_i(x) \big[\mathcal{L}[y_N](x) - f(x)\big]\, dx = 0, \quad i = 1, \ldots, N-1
\]
The trial functions and test functions are identical: $w_i = \phi_i$.

content.text: For a linear operator $\mathcal{L}$, the Galerkin equations become a linear system for the coefficients $c_j$.

content.title: Galerkin with Sine Basis
content.problem:
Solve $y'' + 1 = 0$ with $y(0) = y(\pi) = 0$ using a single sine function.

**Solution.** Let $y_1(x) = c_1 \sin x$. The residual is:
\[
R(x) = y_1''(x) + 1 = -c_1 \sin x + 1
\]

Galerkin condition with $\phi_1(x) = \sin x$:
\[
\int_0^\pi \sin x \cdot (-c_1 \sin x + 1)\, dx = 0
\]

\[
-c_1 \int_0^\pi \sin^2 x\, dx + \int_0^\pi \sin x\, dx = 0
\]

\[
-c_1 \cdot \frac{\pi}{2} + 2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad c_1 = \frac{4}{\pi}
\]

**Solution:** $y_1(x) = \frac{4}{\pi}\sin x \approx 1.273\sin x$.

**Exact solution:** $y = \frac{x(\pi - x)}{2}$.

At $x = \pi/2$: $y_1(\pi/2) = \frac{4}{\pi} \approx 1.273$ vs.\ exact $y(\pi/2) = \frac{\pi^2}{8} \approx 1.234$. Error $\approx 3\%$.

content.text: For second-order BVPs, integration by parts transfers a derivative from the trial function to the test function, reducing smoothness requirements.

content.title: Weak Form of BVP
content.content:
For $-y'' + q(x)y = f(x)$ with $y(a) = y(b) = 0$, the **weak form** is: Find $y$ such that for all test functions $v$ with $v(a) = v(b) = 0$:
\[
\int_a^b y'(x) v'(x)\, dx + \int_a^b q(x) y(x) v(x)\, dx = \int_a^b f(x) v(x)\, dx
\]
This is obtained by multiplying by $v$, integrating, and using integration by parts.

Start with $-y'' + qy = f$. Multiply by test function $v$ and integrate:
\[
-\int_a^b y''(x) v(x)\, dx + \int_a^b q(x) y(x) v(x)\, dx = \int_a^b f(x) v(x)\, dx
\]

Integration by parts on the first term:
\[
-\int_a^b y'' v\, dx = -[y'v]_a^b + \int_a^b y' v'\, dx = \int_a^b y' v'\, dx
\]
since $v(a) = v(b) = 0$.

content.title: Weak Solutions
content.content: A function $y$ satisfying the weak form with $y'$ square-integrable is called a **weak solution**. If $y$ is also twice differentiable, it satisfies the strong (classical) form of the BVP.
content.label: Theorem

content.text: The **finite element method** (FEM) is a Galerkin method using piecewise polynomial basis functions with *local support*.

content.title: Hat Functions
content.content:
On a mesh $a = x_0 < x_1 < \cdots < x_N = b$, the **hat function** (or tent function) $\phi_j$ is:
\[
\phi_j(x) = \begin{cases}
\dfrac{x - x_{j-1}}{x_j - x_{j-1}} & x \in [x_{j-1}, x_j] \\[2ex]
\dfrac{x_{j+1} - x}{x_{j+1} - x_j} & x \in [x_j, x_{j+1}] \\[1ex]
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
\]
Key properties:

- $\phi_j(x_i) = \delta_{ij}$ (interpolation property)

- $\phi_j$ is nonzero only on $[x_{j-1}, x_{j+1}]$ (local support)

- $\sum_{j=0}^N \phi_j(x) = 1$ (partition of unity)

content.title: Properties of Hat Function Basis
content.content: The finite element approximation $y_N(x) = \sum_{j=0}^N c_j \phi_j(x)$ satisfies $y_N(x_j) = c_j$, so the coefficients are the nodal values. The Galerkin equations involve only neighboring nodes due to local support.
content.label: Theorem

content.text: Consider the model problem $-y'' = f$ with $y(0) = y(1) = 0$ on a uniform mesh with $h = 1/N$.

content.title: Stiffness Matrix
content.content:
The weak form is $\int_0^1 y'v'\, dx = \int_0^1 fv\, dx$. With $y_N = \sum_{j=1}^{N-1} c_j\phi_j$ and $v = \phi_i$:
\[
\sum_{j=1}^{N-1} c_j \int_0^1 \phi_j'(x)\phi_i'(x)\, dx = \int_0^1 f(x)\phi_i(x)\, dx
\]
This is the linear system $K\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{f}$ where $K$ is the **stiffness matrix**:
\[
K_{ij} = \int_0^1 \phi_j'(x)\phi_i'(x)\, dx
\]

content.title: Hat Function Stiffness Matrix
content.content:
For hat functions on a uniform mesh with spacing $h$:
\[
K_{ij} = \begin{cases}
\dfrac{2}{h} & i = j \\[1ex]
-\dfrac{1}{h} & |i - j| = 1 \\[1ex]
0 & |i - j| > 1
\end{cases}
\]
The stiffness matrix is tridiagonal:
\[
K = \frac{1}{h}\begin{pmatrix}
2 & -1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
-1 & 2 & -1 & \cdots & 0 \\
0 & -1 & 2 & \ddots & \vdots \\
\vdots & \ddots & \ddots & \ddots & -1 \\
0 & \cdots & 0 & -1 & 2
\end{pmatrix}
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
For adjacent nodes $j$ and $j+1$ on $[x_j, x_{j+1}]$:
\[
\phi_j'(x) = -\frac{1}{h}, \quad \phi_{j+1}'(x) = \frac{1}{h}
\]
So $K_{j,j+1} = \int_{x_j}^{x_{j+1}} \left(-\frac{1}{h}\right)\left(\frac{1}{h}\right)dx = -\frac{1}{h^2} \cdot h = -\frac{1}{h}$.

For diagonal entries:
\[
K_{jj} = \int_{x_{j-1}}^{x_j} \frac{1}{h^2}\, dx + \int_{x_j}^{x_{j+1}} \frac{1}{h^2}\, dx = \frac{2}{h}
\]

content.title: Finite Element Solution
content.problem:
Solve $-y'' = 1$ with $y(0) = y(1) = 0$ using FEM with $N = 4$ elements.

**Solution.** Mesh: $x_j = j/4$ for $j = 0, \ldots, 4$, $h = 0.25$.

The stiffness matrix for interior nodes $j = 1, 2, 3$:
\[
K = 4\begin{pmatrix} 2 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & -1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}
\]

The load vector: $f_i = \int_0^1 1 \cdot \phi_i\, dx = h = 0.25$.
\[
\mathbf{f} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.25 \\ 0.25 \\ 0.25 \end{pmatrix}
\]

Solving $K\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{f}$:
\[
\begin{pmatrix} 8 & -4 & 0 \\ -4 & 8 & -4 \\ 0 & -4 & 8 \end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix} c_1 \\ c_2 \\ c_3 \end{pmatrix}
= \begin{pmatrix} 0.25 \\ 0.25 \\ 0.25 \end{pmatrix}
\]

Solution: $c_1 = c_3 = 0.09375$, $c_2 = 0.125$.

**Exact:** $y = \frac{x(1-x)}{2}$. At $x = 0.25$: exact = $0.09375$. At $x = 0.5$: exact = $0.125$.

The FEM solution matches the exact solution at the nodes! (Superconvergence)

content.text: For efficiency, we compute contributions from each element separately and assemble them into the global system.

content.title: Element Stiffness Matrix
content.content:
On element $[x_{e-1}, x_e]$ with local nodes 1 and 2 (mapped to global nodes $e-1$ and $e$), the local basis functions are:
\[
N_1(\xi) = 1 - \xi, \quad N_2(\xi) = \xi, \quad \xi \in [0,1]
\]
where $\xi = (x - x_{e-1})/h_e$. The **element stiffness matrix** is:
\[
K^{(e)} = \frac{1}{h_e}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
\]

content.title: Finite Element Assembly
content.content:
algorithmic[1]
 Mesh, element stiffness matrices $K^{(e)}$, element load vectors $f^{(e)}$
 Global stiffness matrix $K$, load vector $\mathbf{f}$
 Initialize $K = 0$, $\mathbf{f} = 0$
each element $e = 1, \ldots, N$
 Identify global nodes $(i, j)$ for element $e$
 $K_{ii} \gets K_{ii} + K^{(e)}_{11}$
 $K_{ij} \gets K_{ij} + K^{(e)}_{12}$
 $K_{ji} \gets K_{ji} + K^{(e)}_{21}$
 $K_{jj} \gets K_{jj} + K^{(e)}_{22}$
 $f_i \gets f_i + f^{(e)}_1$
 $f_j \gets f_j + f^{(e)}_2$

 Apply boundary conditions (modify rows/columns)
 $K$, $\mathbf{f}$
algorithmic

content.title: Quadratic Elements
content.content:
Three nodes per element (endpoints plus midpoint) with quadratic Lagrange basis functions:
$$
N_1(\xi) &= 2\xi^2 - 3\xi + 1 \\
N_2(\xi) &= 4\xi(1-\xi) \\
N_3(\xi) &= 2\xi^2 - \xi
$$
Quadratic elements provide $\bigo{h^4}$ accuracy versus $\bigo{h^2}$ for linear elements.

content.title: FEM Error Bounds
content.content:
For the model problem $-y'' = f$ with linear elements:

- **Energy norm error:** $\|y - y_N\|_E = \bigo{h}$ where $\|v\|_E^2 = \int (v')^2\, dx$

- **$L^2$ error:** $\|y - y_N\|_{L^2} = \bigo{h^2}$

- **Nodal error:** $|y(x_j) - y_N(x_j)| = \bigo{h^2}$ (superconvergence)
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Céa's Lemma
content.content:
The Galerkin approximation is quasi-optimal: if $V_h$ is the finite element space,
\[
\|y - y_N\|_E \leq C \inf_{v_h \in V_h} \|y - v_h\|_E
\]
The FEM error is bounded by the best approximation error in the energy norm.
content.label: Theorem
